content.text: Running your LLM across multiple geographic regions simultaneously solves three distinct problems: latency (serve users from nearby compute), availability (region outages don't take down the service), and compliance (keep EU data in the EU). Getting this right for large models requires careful coordination of routing, weight distribution, and failover.

attrs.alt: Multi-region LLM serving architecture spanning US, EU, and APAC

content.text: The diagram shows the canonical multi-region pattern: a global traffic manager routes each request to the nearest healthy region, each region runs independent inference pods backed by locally replicated model weights, and cross-region replication keeps the weight files in sync when you update the model.
Why Multi-Region is Hard for LLMs
Stateless web services trivially replicate across regions. LLMs add complications:
A 70B BF16 model is ~140 GB. Replicating it across 5 regions means 700 GB of storage and hours of sync time on each version bump.
Loading weights into GPU VRAM takes 3–8 minutes for a 70B model, making rapid failover or autoscaling across regions problematic.
Multi-turn conversations that build KV cache are pinned to the instance that holds the cache. You cannot freely route mid-session across regions without losing cache.
H100 capacity is unevenly distributed across providers and regions. Your primary region may have capacity your failover region lacks.
Cross-region or cross-cloud data transfer is expensive (~$0.09/GB on AWS). Long streaming responses sent to the wrong region amplify costs.
GeoDNS resolves your domain to different IP addresses based on the client's location. AWS Route 53 latency routing, Cloudflare, and GCP Cloud DNS all support this.

Cloudflare Workers and GCP's Global External Load Balancer use anycast: the same IP is announced from every edge PoP; BGP routing directs the client to the nearest PoP, which proxies to the closest backend. Benefit: no DNS TTL lag. Tradeoff: you lose direct control over which backend region serves a request.
During a model version rollout, send 5% of traffic to the new version in one region before promoting globally:
The single largest operational challenge is keeping multi-hundred-GB weight files in sync across regions.

After uploading a new model version to the US bucket, replication propagates it to EU and APAC automatically. You then update your pods region by region — a rolling restart that avoids the model vanishing from any region mid-update.

# Kubernetes rolling update — pods load weights from regional S3
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vllm-server
  namespace: llm-prod
spec:
  replicas: 4
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1
      maxSurge: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: vllm
        image: vllm/vllm-openai:v0.5.5
        env:
        - name: MODEL_S3_URI
          value: "s3://models-eu/llama-3-70b/v2.1/"   # bump this to upgrade
        - name: AWS_DEFAULT_REGION
          value: "eu-west-1"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 300   # allow 5 min for weight load
          periodSeconds: 15

When a user is mid-conversation and your service has built KV cache for their prior turns, routing them to a different region would restart the KV cache from scratch, costing extra TTFT. Three approaches:
Load balancer reads X-Region-Affinity header; client repeats it
Client must propagate header; fails on cold reconnect
vLLM prefix cache: rerun common prefixes cheaply
Extra prefill tokens on reconnect; no state to pin
Hash(conversation_id) → region assignment stored in Redis Global
Redis cross-region read adds ~2ms; clean semantics
Drop KV cache on region switch; pay extra prefill
Simplest ops; fine for short conversations
For most LLM APIs the stateless approach is correct: KV cache is an optimization, not a correctness requirement. Rebuild it if the region changes.
Shallow TCP health checks catch crashes but miss GPU OOM or model-loading failures. Use a deep check that generates one token:

Route 53 health checks call this endpoint every 10 seconds. Three consecutive failures remove the region from DNS resolution within the TTL window (60 seconds). This keeps clients from routing to a broken region after a GPU node failure.

# Envoy circuit breaker — trips at 50% error rate over 10s
cluster_name: vllm_cluster
circuit_breakers:
  thresholds:
  - priority: DEFAULT
    max_connections: 1024
    max_pending_requests: 512
    max_retries: 3
outlier_detection:
  consecutive_5xx: 5
  interval: 10s
  base_ejection_time: 30s
  max_ejection_percent: 50
Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, government) and geographies (EU GDPR, China PIPL) often require that user data never leaves a specific region.
Route53 geolocation routing forces EU users to eu-west-1; S3 bucket policy denies cross-region reads
Use AWS GovCloud or dedicated VPC; no shared infra with non-HIPAA tenants
Separate deployment in China region (Ningxia/Beijing); completely isolated control plane
ap-southeast-2 only; prevent failover to other regions for APPI data
Note the critical difference: for latency routing you want
to fail over on outage. For GDPR geo-locked records you want
— better to return 503 than to send EU user data to the US region.
Multi-region deployments multiply your GPU costs. Egress adds on top of that.

Minimize egress by keeping responses short (avoid unnecessary verbosity in system prompts), using response streaming (charges only actual bytes), and routing correctly the first time (avoid cross-region proxying).
Three regions each at 99.9% availability yields
theoretical availability — but only if your routing layer itself has this availability. DNS TTL propagation (60s–300s) and health-check latency (30s–90s) dominate your actual recovery time.
Centralize metrics and traces across regions to spot cross-region anomalies (e.g., APAC latency spike while US is fine).

# Prometheus remote_write to central Thanos/Grafana Cloud
global:
  external_labels:
    region: eu-west-1
    cluster: llm-prod

remote_write:
  - url: https://metrics.example.com/api/v1/push
    headers:
      Authorization: "Bearer ${PROM_PUSH_TOKEN}"
    write_relabel_configs:
    - source_labels: [__name__]
      regex: "vllm_.*|dcgm_.*"
      action: keep

# Grafana dashboard variable — select region
templating:
  list:
  - name: region
    type: query
    datasource: Thanos
    query: label_values(vllm_request_latency_seconds, region)
    multi: true
    includeAll: true
Key cross-region metrics to watch: per-region p50/p99 TTFT, queue depth, GPU utilisation, and error rate. Alert when any single region diverges by more than 2× from the cluster median.
Don't replicate your full US fleet to every region from day 1. APAC may need half the capacity at half the cost. Size each region to its actual traffic.

A model weight corruption or bad config push will hit all regions simultaneously via replication. Add a 1-hour delay on cross-region replication and validate the primary region before replication starts.
GDPR geo-locked records must not fail over to out-of-region. Separate your latency-routed and geo-locked DNS records.

Route 53 checks a load balancer VIP, not individual GPU pods. A single bad pod won't trip the health check. Add Kubernetes readiness probes and PodDisruptionBudgets to catch pod-level failures before they impact users.
A failover that requires loading 140 GB of weights takes 5–8 minutes. Pre-warm standby regions by keeping vLLM pods running (but idle) rather than scaling to zero.
Use per-region API keys with Secrets Manager replication, not a single shared key. A leaked key scoped to one region limits the blast radius.

Multi-region LLM deployment requires a global traffic manager (Route 53 latency routing or Cloudflare), per-region independent inference fleets, S3 cross-region replication for model weights, and careful separation of latency-optimised vs geo-locked routing records. Design for independent regional failure, pre-warm all regions to avoid cold-start failover delays, and never let GDPR geo-locked records fail over to out-of-region endpoints regardless of availability.
